text:
Admission Date:  1997-07-15              Discharge Date:   1996-09-17

Date of Birth:  1991-04-11             Sex:   M

Service: NEUROSURGERY

Allergies:
Percocet

Attending:Thomas
Chief Complaint:
pain in legs with walking

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
L4-5 foramenotomies


History of Present Illness:
As you know, he is a 67-year-old gentleman who presents with low
back pain and difficulty walking more than quarter of a mile.
He has experienced significant decrease over the last year and
particularly over the last six months in terms of his ability to
ambulate.  He also notices pain when standing for prolonged
periods in the back of his legs.  This seems to be cramping in
nature.  Back pain with posterior bending is also noted.  He has
participated in physical therapy for more than six months, but
this has been of only limited help.  He continues to do the
exercises on his own recognizance in the gym.  He denies
difficulty with bowel, bladder, or gait. He is scheduled for B/L
l4-5 laminoforaminotomies.

Past Medical History:
gerd, hypothyroidism, tremor

Social History:
nonsmoker, few drinks per week

Family History:
nc

Physical Exam:
ht rrr
lungs cta
neuro: motor strength is 10-27 in hip flexion,
extension, quadriceps, hamstrings, dorsiflexion, and plantar
flexion bilaterally.  His sensory examination was intact with
respect to the modality of light touch.  His reflexes were
normal
and symmetric.  Straight leg raise was negative bilaterally.
The
pulses were palpable in both feet.

upon discharge:
motor full, incsison cdi


Pertinent Results:
An MRI of the lumbosacral spine demonstrates a grade 1
spondylolisthesis with stenosis at L4-L5.  Flexion and extension
x-rays were obtained which demonstrate no abnormal movement.


Brief Hospital Course:
Pt was admitted electively to hospital, went to OR where under
general anesthesia underwent lumbar decompression.  he tolerated
the procedure well, was extubated, transferred to PACU and then
floor. Diet and actvity were advanced.  Pain medication was
transitioned to PO.  He was voiding without difficulty,
ambulating in halls.  Incision was clean dry and intact. He was
very lethargic on the afternoon of 08-14 and he was transferred
back to the ICU and given Narcan. After Narcan administration he
did become more responsive. Neurology was consulted as he
continued to have arm tremors and was noted to be rigid on
physical exam. They felt this episode was likely related to
dehydration and recommended agressive IVF and continued
monitoring. On 05-10 he was more awake and alert and was
transferred to the floor in stable condition. He was seen by
physical therapy and cleared for home. Prior to DC he was
complaining of a sore throat and on exam he had multiple patchy
areas of exudate on the oropharynx and a culture was sent and he
was started on Amoxicillin 500 q8 for 7 days for strep throat
treatment. He was seen by PT/OT and cleared for discharge to
home with home PT.


Medications on Admission:
- Nexium 40mg daily
- Synthroid 88mcg
- Lexapro 10mg
- MVI
- Caltrate
- Flaxseed oil
- Fish oil


Discharge Medications:
1. levothyroxine 88 mcg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
2. escitalopram 10 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
3. methocarbamol 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO TID (3
times a day).
Disp:*90 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
4. docusate sodium 100 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO BID (2
times a day): take while on pain med.
Disp:*60 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0*
5. tramadol 50 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q4H (every 4
hours) as needed for pain.
Disp:*60 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*
6. amoxicillin 250 mg Capsule Sig: Two (2) Capsule PO Q8H (every
8 hours) for 7 days.
Disp:*21 Capsule(s)* Refills:*0*


Discharge Disposition:
Home With Service

Facility:
SMMC Visiting Nurses

Discharge Diagnosis:
lumbar stenosis
post-op acute urinary retention


Discharge Condition:
AOx3. Activity as tolerated.


Discharge Instructions:
??????        Do not smoke

??????        Keep wound clean / No tub baths or pools until seen in
follow up/ begin daily showers 1982-11-09
??????        You have steri-strips in place ?????? keep dry x 72 hours.
Do not pull them off. They will fall off on their own or be
taken off in the office
??????        No pulling up, lifting> 10 lbs., excessive bending or
twisting for two weeks then increase activity as tolerated to
normal.
??????        Limit your use of stairs to 2-3 times per day
??????        Have a family member check your incision daily for
signs of infection
??????        Take pain medication as instructed; you may find it
best if taken in the a.m. when you wake if you experience muscle
stiffness and before bed for sleeping discomfort
??????        Increase your intake of fluids and fiber as pain
medicine (narcotics) can cause constipation

Followup Instructions:
YOU DO NOT NEED SUTURE REMOVAL. YOU HAVE STERI STRIPS AND THESE
WILL FALL OFF ON THEIR OWN IN 01-19 DAYS.

PLEASE CALL 204-452-3565 TO SCHEDULE AN APPOINTMENT WITH DR.
Briggs TO BE SEEN IN 6 WEEKS.

Please FU with Dr. Gibbs in 04 as previously scheduled.
YOU WILL NOT NEED XRAYS PRIOR TO YOUR APPOINTMENT



Completed by:1997-11-23
